exercises.title: What does /actuator/health return by default?
exercises.scenario: Spring Boot Actuator's health endpoint aggregates component statuses.
exercises.instructions:
A Spring Boot app has: Actuator added, H2 datasource configured, no custom HealthIndicators.

What does GET /actuator/health return? What components appear?
What is the overall status?
What is the default show-details setting?
exercises.expected_solution:
Default response: {"status":"UP"}
Components visible by default: none (details hidden)
With management.endpoint.health.show-details=always: db, diskSpace, ping
Default show-details: never (only top-level status shown)

test_cases.expected:
Default response: {"status":"UP"}
Components visible by default: none (details hidden)
With management.endpoint.health.show-details=always: db, diskSpace, ping
Default show-details: never (only top-level status shown)

hints: Default show-details: never — only {status: UP}
Auto-included: db (if Spring Data), diskSpace, ping
management.endpoint.health.show-details=always to see components

exercises.title: Expose Actuator endpoints and show health details
exercises.scenario: Endpoints must be explicitly exposed; details must be configured.
exercises.instructions: Fill in the application.properties to expose health, info, and metrics with full details.
exercises.starter_code:
# application.properties:

# Expose health, info and metrics:
management.endpoints.web.exposure.______=health,info,metrics

# Show all health component details:
management.endpoint.health.show-_______=always

# App info for /actuator/info:
info.app.______=payment-service
info.app.version=1.0.0

blanks.hint: Property to include endpoints in web exposure

hints: management.endpoints.web.exposure.include: whitelist of exposed endpoints
show-details=always: components visible without authentication

exercises.title: Minimal custom HealthIndicator
exercises.scenario: A custom HealthIndicator checks if an external dependency is up.
exercises.instructions:
Write a `PaymentGatewayHealthIndicator` that:
1. Implements HealthIndicator
2. In health(): returns Health.up() if a boolean `isGatewayReachable()` returns true
3. Returns Health.down() with detail 'gateway unreachable' if false
4. Is a @Component (auto-registered by Actuator)
hints: Health.up().withDetail('key', value).build()
Health.down().withDetail('reason', '...').build()
@Component: Spring registers this automatically with Actuator

exercises.title: Micrometer Counter — count events
exercises.scenario: Counter increments on each event occurrence.
exercises.instructions:
Write an `OrderService` that:
1. Injects MeterRegistry via constructor
2. Creates a Counter 'orders.placed' in the constructor
3. Creates a Counter 'orders.failed' with tag 'reason' to be set at increment time
4. Increments appropriate counter in placeOrder() and on exception

Note: with tags set at increment time, use registry.counter('orders.failed', 'reason', 'validation').
hints: Counter.builder('name').description('').tag('k','v').register(registry)
counter.increment(): add 1 to the counter
registry.counter('name', 'tag', 'value'): counter with tag set at use time

exercises.title: Micrometer Gauge — track current state
exercises.scenario: Gauge reflects a current value (queue depth, active connections).
exercises.instructions:
Write a `PaymentQueueService` that:
1. Uses AtomicInteger to track pending payments count
2. Registers it as a Gauge named 'payments.pending'
3. Has enqueue() incrementing and dequeue() decrementing the counter
4. The Gauge automatically reflects the AtomicInteger value
hints: registry.gauge('name', atomicInteger): Gauge reads AI value automatically
Gauge = current snapshot (can go up/down), Counter = always increasing
AtomicInteger is thread-safe — important for concurrent queue access

exercises.title: Micrometer Timer — measure operation duration
exercises.scenario: Timer records count, total time, and percentiles.
exercises.instructions:
Write a `DatabaseService` that:
1. Wraps each query in a Timer named 'db.query.duration' with tag 'query'
2. Uses Timer.record(Supplier<T>) for findById
3. Uses Timer.start/stop for a more complex findAll operation
4. Prints the timer stats: count and total time
hints: Timer.record(supplier): measures duration + returns supplier result
Timer.start(registry) + sample.stop(timer): manual measurement
timer.count(): number of recordings; timer.totalTime(MILLISECONDS): sum

exercises.title: @SpringBootTest — verify /actuator/health
exercises.scenario: Integration test that the health endpoint returns UP.
exercises.instructions:
Write a @SpringBootTest(RANDOM_PORT) test that:
1. Calls GET /actuator/health via TestRestTemplate
2. Asserts status code is 200
3. Asserts response body contains '"status":"UP"'
4. Calls GET /actuator/info and asserts it contains the app name
hints: @SpringBootTest RANDOM_PORT: starts real server with actuator endpoints
TestRestTemplate: doesn't throw on non-2xx — use assertEquals on status
management.endpoints.web.exposure.include=health,info in test properties

exercises.title: HealthIndicator with DOWN state
exercises.scenario: Health indicators should accurately reflect dependency health.
exercises.instructions:
Write `StockFeedHealthIndicator` that:
1. Depends on a StockFeedClient with isConnected() and getLatencyMs() methods
2. Returns Health.up() with latency detail if connected
3. Returns Health.down() with 'Stock feed disconnected' if not
4. Returns Health.unknown() if latency > 500ms (connected but slow)
5. Write @SpringBootTest verifying all three states
hints: Health.unknown(): between UP and DOWN — degraded state
.withDetail('key', value): add diagnostic info
Test with mock client — no Spring context needed for unit testing HealthIndicator

exercises.title: Verify metrics increment in tests
exercises.scenario: MeterRegistry can be inspected in tests to assert metric values.
exercises.instructions:
Write a @SpringBootTest test for OrderService that:
1. @Autowired MeterRegistry and OrderService
2. Calls placeOrder() 3 times (valid)
3. Asserts orders.placed counter = 3
4. Calls placeOrder() with invalid data (throws)
5. Asserts orders.failed counter = 1
hints: registry.find('metric.name').counter(): find a counter in the registry
counter.count(): current count as double
Measure delta (after - before) to avoid test order dependency

exercises.title: What happens when a HealthIndicator throws?
exercises.scenario: Uncaught exceptions in health() affect the overall health status.
exercises.expected_solution:
The BrokenIndicator component status becomes DOWN
Spring Actuator catches the exception and treats it as DOWN
Overall status: DOWN (because one component is DOWN)
Response: {"status":"DOWN","components":{"broken":{"status":"DOWN","details":{"error":"Connection pool exhausted!"}}}}
Note: the exception message appears in the 'error' detail field

test_cases.expected:
The BrokenIndicator component status becomes DOWN
Spring Actuator catches the exception and treats it as DOWN
Overall status: DOWN (because one component is DOWN)
Response: {"status":"DOWN","components":{"broken":{"status":"DOWN","details":{"error":"Connection pool exhausted!"}}}}
Note: the exception message appears in the 'error' detail field

hints: Actuator wraps each HealthIndicator.health() call in try-catch
Exception → component status DOWN → overall DOWN
Never let health() throw — return Health.down() instead, use try-catch

exercises.title: Cached HealthIndicator — don't check on every request
exercises.scenario: Cache the health result to avoid hitting external services per probe.
exercises.instructions:
Write a `CachedExternalApiHealthIndicator` that:
1. Actually checks an external API only every 30 seconds (@Scheduled)
2. Caches the result in an AtomicReference<Health>
3. HealthIndicator.health() returns the cached result instantly
4. Starts as Health.unknown() until first check completes
hints: AtomicReference<Health>: thread-safe holder for the cached Health object
@Scheduled runs the check; health() reads the cache — no blocking
Initial UNKNOWN: reasonable until first @Scheduled run completes

exercises.title: Change log level at runtime via Actuator
exercises.instructions:
Write a @SpringBootTest(RANDOM_PORT) test that:
1. Gets the current log level for 'com.codequest' via GET /actuator/loggers/com.codequest
2. Changes it to DEBUG via POST /actuator/loggers/com.codequest
3. Verifies the level changed via GET
4. Resets to INFO

Note: management.endpoints.web.exposure.include must include 'loggers'.
hints: POST /actuator/loggers/{logger}: body = {configuredLevel: DEBUG}
Returns 204 No Content on success
GET /actuator/loggers/{logger}: returns {configuredLevel, effectiveLevel}

exercises.title: Full observability stack — health + metrics + info
exercises.scenario: Production-ready observability on a payment service.
exercises.instructions:
Build complete observability for PaymentService:
1. `PaymentGatewayHealthIndicator`: checks ping, shows latency, DOWN if >500ms
2. `PaymentMetrics` @Component: Counter payments.processed, Counter payments.failed, Gauge payments.queued, Timer payment.processing.duration
3. PaymentService.process() uses all 4 metrics
4. application.properties: expose health,info,metrics
5. @SpringBootTest(RANDOM_PORT): verify /actuator/health UP, /actuator/info has version, /actuator/metrics lists payment.* metrics
exercises.starter_code: // Full observability: HealthIndicator + 4 Micrometer metrics + properties + test
hints: @Component PaymentMetrics: inject into PaymentService via constructor
metrics.processingTimer.record(Callable): wraps operation and records time
GET /actuator/metrics: returns list of all registered metric names

exercises.title: Kubernetes-ready liveness and readiness probes
exercises.scenario: Spring Boot Actuator provides separate liveness and readiness groups.
exercises.instructions:
Configure the application to expose:
1. /actuator/health/liveness — always UP unless JVM is broken
2. /actuator/health/readiness — DOWN when DB or payment gateway is not ready

Write application.properties configuration. Write a ReadinessIndicator that goes DOWN if a required service is not initialized. Write @SpringBootTest verifying both paths.
hints: management.endpoint.health.probes.enabled=true: adds /health/liveness and /health/readiness
/health/liveness: JVM alive; /health/readiness: ready to serve
@EventListener ContextRefreshedEvent: fires after all beans initialized

exercises.title: Complete production observability — Module 14.5 synthesis
exercises.scenario: Full Actuator stack: health, metrics, info, secured endpoints.
exercises.instructions:
Build production-ready observability for the Zerodha-style trading service:
1. `MarketFeedHealthIndicator`: UP with latency if connected, DOWN if not, cached with @Scheduled
2. `TradingMetrics`: Counter trades.executed (with 'symbol' tag), Timer trade.execution.duration, Gauge positions.open
3. `TradingService.executeTrade()`: uses all metrics, wrapped in Timer
4. application.properties: expose health,info,metrics; secure env and threaddump; show details; info.version
5. @SpringBootTest(RANDOM_PORT): 4 tests — health UP, metrics listed, info has version, trade increments counter

All 4 tests must pass.
hints: Cached HealthIndicator: AtomicReference<Health> updated by @Scheduled, health() reads cache
Timer.record(Callable<T>): wraps and returns the result while timing
registry.find('metric.name').counter(): look up a counter by name in tests
